title: Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease
text:
Curated Clinical Review: Effect of an Enteric-Coated Fish-Oil Preparation on Relapses in Crohn’s Disease

Andrea Belluzzi, Corrado Brignola, Massimo Campieri, Angelo Perà, Stefano Boschi, and Mario Miglioli. New England Journal of Medicine. 1996;334(24):1557-1560. doi:10.1056/NEJM199606133342401

Background & Rationale
Crohn’s disease is characterised by periods of remission interrupted by relapses, with the highest risk occurring soon after remission is achieved. Patients with elevated acute-phase proteins have an increased risk of relapse. Fish oil has demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties through mechanisms including reduced production of leukotriene B4 and thromboxane A2, inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines, and free radical scavenging. However, conventional fish oil preparations are poorly tolerated due to gastrointestinal side effects and unpleasant taste. This study evaluated a novel enteric-coated fish-oil preparation designed to improve tolerability while maintaining therapeutic efficacy.

Study Design
This was a one-year, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial conducted between May 1992 and September 1993 at two Italian centres. Seventy-eight patients with established Crohn’s disease in clinical remission were enrolled. Eligibility required a Crohn’s Disease Activity Index score below 150 for at least three months but less than two years, plus evidence of inflammation (elevated serum a1-acid glycoprotein, a2-globulin, or erythrocyte sedimentation rate). Key exclusion criteria included recent use of mesalamine, sulfasalazine, corticosteroids, or immunosuppressive drugs, and extensive bowel resection. Patients were randomised to receive either nine enteric-coated capsules daily containing 2.7 g of n-3 fatty acids (1.8 g eicosapentaenoic acid and 0.9 g docosahexaenoic acid) or identical placebo capsules. The primary endpoint was clinical relapse, defined as an increase in Crohn’s Disease Activity Index score by at least 100 points above baseline and above 150 for more than two weeks. Secondary endpoints included maintenance of remission and changes in inflammatory markers. Randomisation was balanced-block, and blinding was maintained throughout. Statistical analysis used chi-square tests, Kaplan-Meier survival curves, log-rank analysis, and multivariate logistic regression.

Patient Population
The study population comprised 78 patients (39 per group) with a median age of 34-39 years and a male-to-female ratio of approximately 1:1. The median duration of disease was 66-68 months, and the median duration of remission was 7-8 months. Previous bowel resection had been performed in 13-14 patients per group. Baseline characteristics including disease location, smoking status, and Crohn’s Disease Activity Index scores were similar between groups. All patients had evidence of inflammation at baseline, placing them at high risk for relapse.

Key Findings
The relapse rate was significantly lower in the fish-oil group compared to placebo: 11 of 39 patients (28%) versus 27 of 39 patients (69%), representing a 41 percentage point difference (95% CI: 21 to 61; P=0.001). After one year, 23 patients (59%) in the fish-oil group remained in remission compared to 10 patients (26%) in the placebo group (P=0.003). Kaplan-Meier analysis confirmed significantly better maintenance of remission in the fish-oil group (P=0.006). Multivariate logistic regression identified fish-oil treatment as the only significant predictor of relapse (odds ratio 4.2; 95% CI: 1.6 to 10.7), with sex, age, previous surgery, disease duration, and smoking status having no significant effect. Among patients who relapsed, the distribution of disease location and major symptoms were similar between groups.

Safety & Tolerability
Five patients (10%) in the fish-oil group discontinued due to diarrhoea, which began within the first month of treatment and did not improve with dose reduction. One patient in each group was lost to follow-up. No other side effects were reported. Laboratory markers of inflammation (erythrocyte sedimentation rate, serum a2-globulins, and serum a1-acid glycoprotein) showed significant improvement in the fish-oil group compared to placebo. Analysis of red blood cell phospholipid fatty acid composition demonstrated incorporation of n-3 fatty acids and displacement of arachidonic acid in the fish-oil group.

Authors' Conclusions
The authors concluded that the enteric-coated fish-oil preparation significantly reduced the rate of relapse in patients with Crohn’s disease in remission. They noted that the preparation was well-tolerated by most patients and that the high degree of n-3 fatty acid absorption and incorporation into phospholipid membranes contributed to its efficacy. The authors acknowledged that the relative efficacy of this preparation compared to mesalamine, the current standard treatment for maintenance of remission, remains to be determined.

Reference
Belluzzi A, Brignola C, Campieri M, Perà A, Boschi S, Miglioli M. Effect of an enteric-coated fish-oil preparation on relapses in Crohn’s disease. N Engl J Med. 1996;334(24):1557-1560. doi:10.1056/NEJM199606133342401
